name: Mount Fuji
summary: Mount Fuji is an active stratovolcano located on the border between Shizuoka and Yamanashi prefectures in Japan. Standing at 3,776 meters, it is the highest peak in Japan and one of the country's most iconic symbols. The mountain has been a sacred site for centuries and has inspired countless works of art and literature.

timeline.title: First Recorded Eruption
timeline.description: The first historically recorded eruption of Mount Fuji occurred in 781 AD during the Nara period. This eruption marked the beginning of the mountain's documented volcanic activity and its significance in Japanese history.

timeline.title: Jōgan Eruption
timeline.description: The devastating Jōgan eruption lasted for 10 days and created the Aokigahara forest on the northwest side of the mountain. The lava flows also formed the Fuji Five Lakes, which are now popular tourist destinations.

timeline.title: Hōei Eruption
timeline.description: The last major eruption of Mount Fuji occurred during the Hōei period, lasting for two weeks. Volcanic ash reached Edo (modern Tokyo), approximately 100 kilometers away. This eruption significantly altered the mountain's appearance.

timeline.title: Edo Period Pilgrimages
timeline.description: During the Edo period (1603-1868), Mount Fuji became a major pilgrimage site. The practice of 'Fuji-ko' (Fuji worship) became popular, with thousands of pilgrims climbing the mountain annually. The first climbing routes were established during this time.

timeline.title: Opening to Foreign Visitors
timeline.description: Following the Meiji Restoration, Japan opened its borders to foreigners. Mount Fuji became accessible to international visitors, and the first foreign climbers began to document and photograph the mountain.

timeline.title: Fuji-Hakone-Izu National Park
timeline.description: Mount Fuji and its surrounding area were designated as Fuji-Hakone-Izu National Park, one of Japan's first national parks. This designation helped protect the mountain's natural environment and promoted tourism.

timeline.title: UNESCO World Heritage Site
timeline.description: Mount Fuji was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site under the category of 'Cultural Site' rather than 'Natural Site,' recognizing its profound influence on Japanese art, literature, and spiritual culture over centuries.

The Hōei eruption with ash clouds rising from the summit
